query: What are the air quality trends in the city?

response.headline: Air Pollution Has Dropped Sharply Since the 1990s
response.lede: Many residents worry about the air they breathe, especially during still winter days. But the long-term numbers offer some good news: air pollution levels at Grensásvegur have dropped by about 75% since their peak in 1994.

story_blocks.body: Air quality has improved a lot since the mid-90s, with pollution levels now a quarter of what they used to be.

story_blocks.heading: A Sharp Drop After the 90s
story_blocks.body: In the chart below, you can see how air pollution peaked in the 1990s, then fell steeply. By 2016, total concentrations were down from 134 to 34 micrograms per cubic meter—a huge improvement. Look for the steep decline after the early years.

story_blocks.heading: Ozone Is Going Up, Others Are Down
story_blocks.body: The next chart compares all main pollutants. Most harmful substances—like nitrogen dioxide, carbon monoxide, and dust—have dropped since the mid-90s. Ozone, however, is showing the opposite trend and has risen over time. You’ll see this gap clearly in the bar chart, where ozone stands out as the only one going up.

story_blocks.heading: Some Pollutants Varied Over the Years
story_blocks.body: See the heatmap below to get a feel for which years and which pollutants hit their high points. Nitrogen dioxide and dust were once much higher, but their hot spots fade in recent years. Ozone's steady climb also shows up here, staying strong even as others drop off.

story_blocks.heading: Seasons Matter—But the Trend Remains Clear
story_blocks.body: The area chart shows how pollution can rise and fall depending on the season or year, but the biggest story is the steady progress overall. Air is cleaner now than it was twenty years ago, even if there are some ups and downs along the way.

story_blocks.heading: What This Means for Your Health
story_blocks.body: Breathing cleaner air helps everyone—especially children, the elderly, and people with allergies or asthma. Even though ozone has gone up, the big drop in other pollutants means Reykjavík’s air is much safer today than a generation ago. It's a reminder of how long-term efforts can make a real difference in daily life.

response.data_note: This story is based on air quality measurements from Grensásvegur, covering the years 1994 to 2016. The numbers show averages across all pollutants recorded at this station; your local air may differ. Ozone readings were not available for 2010 and may be unreliable in 2016. The figures come from Reykjavík's environmental department, but no official licence information is provided, so this public summary is for general information only.
